APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses semantic role labeling using deep BiLSTM architectures and constrained decoding with strong empirical gains on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses methods, baselines, and evaluation metrics (F1, predicate-level correctness) expected for the community, reports hyperparameters and releases code, and situates itself against relevant prior work, making it well positioned for a top conference audience. | B(5): This work squarely targets a core NLP task (semantic role labeling) and presents methods (deep highway BiLSTMs, constrained A* decoding, PoE ensemble) and evaluations (F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012, comparison to established baselines) that are standard and expected at top NLP/ML venues. It reports strong state-of-the-art results (83.2/83.4 F1), releases code, provides hyperparameters, and situates itself within ongoing syntax-vs.-syntax-free modeling debates, so the audience, problem formulation, evaluation standards, and positioning are all appropriate for a top conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses semantic role labeling using deep BiLSTM architectures and constrained decoding with strong empirical gains on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses methods, baselines, and evaluation metrics (F1, predicate-level correctness) expected for the community, reports hyperparameters and releases code, and situates itself against relevant prior work, making it well positioned for a top conference audience.

It reports strong state-of-the-art results (83.2/83.4 F1), releases code, provides hyperparameters, and situates itself within ongoing syntax-vs.-syntax-free modeling debates, so the audience, problem formulation, evaluation standards, and positioning are all appropriate for a top conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (e.g., Zhou & Xu 2015, FitzGerald et al. 2015, Täckström et al. 2015, Choe & Charniak 2016) and evaluates on the standard CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 benchmarks using F1 and predicate-level metrics. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where syntactic methods give comparable or better results and when enforcing constraints hurts performance. The manuscript also analyzes where its model differs from prior work (long-distance argument recovery, constraint violations) and reports thorough related-work coverage. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (e.g., Zhou & Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015; Choe & Charniak 2016) using standard datasets (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012) and metrics (F1, % completely correct predicates). Comparisons appear fair (same data and eval metrics), the authors acknowledge when prior methods give comparable or small improvements (e.g., effects of syntactic parsers like Choe/Charniak), and they analyze key differences (impact on long-distance dependencies, constraint violations), with thorough related-work coverage.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where syntactic methods give comparable or better results and when enforcing constraints hurts performance. The manuscript also analyzes where its model differs from prior work (long-distance argument recovery, constraint violations) and reports thorough related-work coverage.

Comparisons appear fair (same data and eval metrics), the authors acknowledge when prior methods give comparable or small improvements (e.g., effects of syntactic parsers like Choe/Charniak), and they analyze key differences (impact on long-distance dependencies, constraint violations), with thorough related-work coverage.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses a well-studied SRL problem and primarily combines and scales known techniques (stacked highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization, constrained A* decoding, and a product-of-experts ensemble) rather than proposing a fundamentally new paradigm. It clearly differentiates its contributions (e.g., an 8-layer PoE achieving 83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012 and analysis showing deep models better recover long-distance dependencies and reduce reliance on syntax). These are meaningful, non-trivial engineering and empirical advances that would inform experts, but overall the work is an incremental improvement rather than a radical new idea. | B(3): The paper applies known components (stacked highway BiLSTMs with recurrent dropout and orthonormal initialization, constrained A* decoding, and a product-of-experts ensemble) to span-based SRL and demonstrates strong empirical gains on CoNLL-2005/2012. The problem (semantic role labeling) is well-studied, and the methods appear to be a non-trivial but incremental combination of existing techniques rather than a wholly new algorithmic idea. The clear differentiation from prior work and the empirical finding that deep models recover long-distance dependencies and reduce reliance on syntax are informative to experts, but overall the novelty is moderate rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied SRL problem and primarily combines and scales known techniques (stacked highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal initialization, constrained A* decoding, and a product-of-experts ensemble) rather than proposing a fundamentally new paradigm. It clearly differentiates its contributions (e.g., an 8-layer PoE achieving 83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012 and analysis showing deep models better recover long-distance dependencies and reduce reliance on syntax). The problem (semantic role labeling) is well-studied, and the methods appear to be a non-trivial but incremental combination of existing techniques rather than a wholly new algorithmic idea. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem (semantic role labeling) and demonstrates substantive empirical gains — an ~10% relative error reduction with an 8-layer BiLSTM ensemble achieving 83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012 — which should change perceptions about the necessity of explicit syntax for high SRL performance. By showing deep models better recover long-distance predicate-argument relations and by combining constrained A* decoding and PoE ensembling, it points to concrete modeling and decoding directions likely to be adopted and extended. Remaining issues (adjunct/PP attachment errors, global constraint violations, distance-based degradation) are clearly identified, making this a strong-impact contribution that opens useful follow-up work rather than a narrow or purely incremental result. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem (span-based SRL) and delivers a substantial empirical advance: an 8-layer ensemble achieving 83.2 F1 on CoNLL-2005 and 83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2012—about a 10% relative error reduction over prior state of the art—using deep highway BiLSTMs with constrained decoding. It changes how researchers view the role of syntax by showing strong end-to-end performance and improved recovery of long-distance predicate-argument dependencies, while also clarifying when and how syntactic information still helps. The work is likely to be widely built upon (code released, clear gains) and opens productive directions for deeper recurrent architectures and constrained decoding, though some limitations remain (adjunct/PP attachment errors and constraint violations).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem (semantic role labeling) and demonstrates substantive empirical gains — an ~10% relative error reduction with an 8-layer BiLSTM ensemble achieving 83.2/83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2005/2012 — which should change perceptions about the necessity of explicit syntax for high SRL performance. By showing deep models better recover long-distance predicate-argument relations and by combining constrained A* decoding and PoE ensembling, it points to concrete modeling and decoding directions likely to be adopted and extended. Remaining issues (adjunct/PP attachment errors, global constraint violations, distance-based degradation) are clearly identified, making this a strong-impact contribution that opens useful follow-up work rather than a narrow or purely incremental result.

The work is likely to be widely built upon (code released, clear gains) and opens productive directions for deeper recurrent architectures and constrained decoding, though some limitations remain (adjunct/PP attachment errors and constraint violations).

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized (standard intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (deep BiLSTM + constrained decoding, strong ensemble results). Notation and methodological details seem adequately defined (highway BiLSTMs, recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, A* decoding) and hyperparameters and code are reported, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and comparisons (CoNLL results, baselines) appear informative, and overall writing quality is described as good, though some nuanced discussion of constraint types (R constraints) suggests minor presentation complexity that could be clarified. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear methodological thread (deep BiLSTM model description, constrained decoding, datasets/baselines, and evaluation), and its main contributions and quantitative claims (e.g., 83.2/83.4 F1, ~10% relative error reduction) are stated early. Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and transparent (hyperparameters reported, code available, thorough related-work coverage). Results and comparisons to baselines are presented clearly (F1 and predicate-correct metrics), and the writing quality is described as good; these justify a high but not perfect clarity score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized (standard intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (deep BiLSTM + constrained decoding, strong ensemble results). reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear methodological thread (deep BiLSTM model description, constrained decoding, datasets/baselines, and evaluation), and its main contributions and quantitative claims (e.g., 83.2/83.4 F1, ~10% relative error reduction) are stated early. Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and transparent (hyperparameters reported, code available, thorough related-work coverage). SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly supported by empirical evidence: extensive experiments on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 report 83.2/83.4 F1 and comparisons to appropriate baselines (including syntax-based systems), and detailed analyses (long-distance dependencies, constraint effects) are reported. There are no apparent mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound (code and hyperparameters provided, meaningful baselines used). A minor technical weakness is that statistical significance testing is not mentioned and large ensemble gains (PoE over 8 models) may obscure how much improvement comes from architecture versus ensembling. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results. | B(4): The main claims are well supported by empirical evidence: experiments on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 against strong baselines show the reported gains (83.2/83.4 F1 for the ensemble) and the paper analyzes failure modes (PP attachment/adjuncts) and constraint effects. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound with appropriate baselines, code and hyperparameters provided. Minor issues remain (no mention of statistical significance testing and some conclusions about the role of syntax may be dataset- or parser-dependent), but these do not undermine the core technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly supported by empirical evidence: extensive experiments on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 report 83.2/83.4 F1 and comparisons to appropriate baselines (including syntax-based systems), and detailed analyses (long-distance dependencies, constraint effects) are reported. There are no apparent mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound (code and hyperparameters provided, meaningful baselines used). A minor technical weakness is that statistical significance testing is not mentioned and large ensemble gains (PoE over 8 models) may obscure how much improvement comes from architecture versus ensembling. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are well supported by empirical evidence: experiments on CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012 against strong baselines show the reported gains (83.2/83.4 F1 for the ensemble) and the paper analyzes failure modes (PP attachment/adjuncts) and constraint effects. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology appears sound with appropriate baselines, code and hyperparameters provided. Minor issues remain (no mention of statistical significance testing and some conclusions about the role of syntax may be dataset- or parser-dependent), but these do not undermine the core technical claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong and broadly convincing: evaluation on CoNLL-2005/2012, comparisons to many prior systems, and detailed ensemble (PoE) results supporting the main claims. The paper includes useful sensitivity analyses (effects of enforcing SRL constraints, impact of using syntactic parses, and decay with argument distance), but the summary does not clearly report systematic ablations isolating each architectural choice (e.g., highway layers, orthonormal init, recurrent dropout). Related work coverage is described as thorough, failure modes and negative results are explicitly discussed (PP attachment, adjunct distinction, constraint violations), and reproducibility is well supported (code and hyperparameters released). | B(4): The paper presents comprehensive experiments across CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012, compares to a wide set of baselines, reports F1 and predicate-level metrics, and achieves strong ensemble results, supporting the main claims. The authors include sensitivity analyses (effect of depth on long-distance arguments, constraint enforcement effects, and impact of using syntax) and discuss failure modes (adjunct/PP attachment, constraint violations), while providing code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor gap is that the summary does not explicitly report ablations for every architectural choice (e.g., individual contributions of recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, or highway layers), so it stops short of being exhaustively analyzed.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are strong and broadly convincing: evaluation on CoNLL-2005/2012, comparisons to many prior systems, and detailed ensemble (PoE) results supporting the main claims. The paper includes useful sensitivity analyses (effects of enforcing SRL constraints, impact of using syntactic parses, and decay with argument distance), but the summary does not clearly report systematic ablations isolating each architectural choice (e.g., highway layers, orthonormal init, recurrent dropout). Related work coverage is described as thorough, failure modes and negative results are explicitly discussed (PP attachment, adjunct distinction, constraint violations), and reproducibility is well supported (code and hyperparameters released).

The authors include sensitivity analyses (effect of depth on long-distance arguments, constraint enforcement effects, and impact of using syntax) and discuss failure modes (adjunct/PP attachment, constraint violations), while providing code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough. A minor gap is that the summary does not explicitly report ablations for every architectural choice (e.g., individual contributions of recurrent dropout, orthonormal init, or highway layers), so it stops short of being exhaustively analyzed.

main_claims: A deep highway BiLSTM model with constrained decoding significantly improves state-of-the-art span-based SRL performance.
Their 8-layer ensemble (PoE) achieves 83.2 F1 on CoNLL-2005 and 83.4 F1 on CoNLL-2012, roughly a 10% relative error reduction over prior state of the art.
Deep models excel at recovering long-distance predicate-argument dependencies, reducing the gap versus syntax-based models.
Although strong end-to-end performance is possible without explicit syntax, accurate syntactic information can still further improve results.
_summary.methodology: Train deep (stacked) highway bidirectional LSTMs with recurrent dropout and orthonormal initialization to produce local tag scores, and perform constrained A* decoding (with BIO and selected SRL/syntactic constraints); ensemble predictions via a product-of-experts over multiple models.
limitations: Model still makes many errors on adjunct distinction, often tied to prepositional phrase (PP) attachment errors.
Model predictions can violate SRL-specific global constraints; some constraint types (R constraints) are commonly violated in gold data and enforcing them harms performance.
Performance degrades for arguments farther from the predicate, though deep models deteriorate less than shallow ones.
Using syntactic parses can yield only small improvements or hurt performance if the model over-relies on them (improvement from Choe's parser small; Charniak parser can hurt).
